title: Non Freudian Psychology
wikitext:
[]: Non-Freudian Psychology             Quote Post by Trajan » Fri Apr 15, 2011 9:38 pm Everyone always mentions all the Freudian and Jungian psychological components in Evangelion; however, while these two may be the most prominent of the psychological influences in Eva, they are hardly the only ones. Carl Rogers and Humanistic and Cognitive Psychology are almost as important to the themes of the series as the Psychoanalytic stuff IMO. In short here's a quick list of stuff I found that I feel is relevant to the series: Self-Actualization Psychology of Self Self-Efficacy Basically, this is a thread to talk about the less discussed psychological allusions within Eva.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Apr 16, 2011 12:26 am With the exception of Self-Efficacy, Jung wrote on both Self-Realization (which seems very close to your link on Actualization) and the phenomenology of self. The Self-Efficacy bit might be something new and interesting, though.

Article: Non Freudian Psychology
[]: Non-Freudian Psychology             Quote Post by Trajan » Fri Apr 15, 2011 9:38 pm Everyone always mentions all the Freudian and Jungian psychological components in Evangelion; however, while these two may be the most prominent of the psychological influences in Eva, they are hardly the only ones. Carl Rogers and Humanistic and Cognitive Psychology are almost as important to the themes of the series as the Psychoanalytic stuff IMO. In short here's a quick list of stuff I found that I feel is relevant to the series: Self-Actualization Psychology of Self Self-Efficacy Basically, this is a thread to talk about the less discussed psychological allusions within Eva.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Apr 16, 2011 12:26 am With the exception of Self-Efficacy, Jung wrote on both Self-Realization (which seems very close to your link on Actualization) and the phenomenology of self. The Self-Efficacy bit might be something new and interesting, though.
